title: Transformations
primer: In a fragment shader there are no vertex transforms — instead you transform the UV coordinate itself before sampling or evaluating a shape. A 2×2 rotation matrix rotates UV space around a pivot; subtracting an offset translates it; scaling multiplies. These inverse transforms — applied to coordinates rather than geometry — are the foundation of panning, zooming, rotating, and tiling in 2D shaders.

challenges.title: Translate UV space
challenges.description: Shift UV space so that the origin moves to the canvas center, making (0,0) the center pixel. Then draw a white circle of radius 0.25 centered at the new origin. This is the first step before any rotation or zoom.
hints: Subtracting a constant from UV coordinates translates the coordinate system. To move the origin to the center, subtract `vec2(0.5)` — the current center position.
After translation, `p = vec2(0, 0)` corresponds to the canvas center, and `length(p)` gives the distance from center — which is exactly what you need for a centered circle.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Translation in UV space is just vector subtraction. To draw something at position P, you subtract P from UV — this shifts all coordinates so that the point at UV=P now maps to UV=0, where your shape formula is defined. This inversion (transform the space, not the shape) is a fundamental GPU pattern: you always work in canonical space and move the space to the shape.

challenges.title: Rotate a rectangle
challenges.description: Apply a 2D rotation matrix to UV space to spin a rectangle over time. Build a mat2 rotation matrix using cos and sin of u_time, multiply the centered UV by the matrix, then draw a white rectangle.
hints: The 2D rotation matrix is `[[cos θ, -sin θ], [sin θ, cos θ]]`. In GLSL column-major order: `mat2(cos, sin, -sin, cos)` or equivalently `mat2(c, s, -s, c)` — check your column ordering. The standard form is `mat2(c, -s, s, c)` which means column 0 = (c, s), column 1 = (-s, c).

GLSL `mat2` is column-major: `mat2(c, -s, s, c)` constructs columns [(c, -s), (s, c)] — which is the transpose of the mathematical form. Use `mat2(c, s, -s, c)` to get the correct counter-clockwise rotation.
challenges.unlocks_theory: GLSL `mat2` is column-major: `mat2(a, b, c, d)` means the first column is (a, b) and the second column is (c, d). The counter-clockwise rotation matrix with columns (cos θ, sin θ) and (-sin θ, cos θ) is written `mat2(c, s, -s, c)`. Forgetting column-major order is the most common source of incorrect rotations in GLSL.

hlsl.note: HLSL matrices are row-major by default, so the rotation matrix entries are transposed relative to GLSL.

challenges.title: Zoom (scale UV)
challenges.description: Create a zoom effect by scaling UV space. Multiplying centered UV coordinates by a value > 1 zooms out; dividing (or multiplying by < 1) zooms in. Animate the zoom with u_time so the circle pulses between 0.5× and 2× zoom.
hints: Scaling UV coordinates by a factor `zoom` is equivalent to zooming the view. When `zoom > 1`, the shape appears smaller (zoomed out); when `zoom < 1`, it appears larger (zoomed in).
Oscillate with: `float zoom = 1.25 + sin(u_time) * 0.75;` — this gives a range of [0.5, 2.0]. Then apply it with `p = p * zoom;`.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Scaling in UV space is multiplicative. Multiplying `p` by a scale factor > 1 stretches space (shape looks smaller); multiplying by < 1 compresses space (shape looks bigger). The scale factor lives in the coordinate transform, not in the shape definition — so you can zoom, pan, and rotate any shape with no changes to the SDF itself.

challenges.title: Kaleidoscope
challenges.description: Create a 6-fold kaleidoscope by converting to polar coordinates, using mod on the angle to fold space into repeating wedges, then rotating slowly with time. The canvas is filled with a mirrored wedge of a circle-ring pattern repeated 6 times.
hints: After `a = mod(a, 2.0 * slice)`, the angle is folded into a repeating range [0, 2*slice]. Mirror it by reflecting at the midpoint: `a = abs(a - slice)`. Now all 6 wedges show the same mirrored pattern.
Reconstruct a Cartesian coordinate from the modified polar angle: `p = vec2(cos(a), sin(a)) * r`. This gives you a position within the canonical wedge that you can use to draw shapes normally.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Kaleidoscopes exploit the symmetry of polar coordinates. By taking the angle modulo a wedge size and then reflecting it, you map the entire plane onto a single canonical wedge — any shape drawn in that wedge is automatically repeated and mirrored N times. This is the foundation of symmetry-based generative art and can be combined with any SDF or texture.

hlsl.note: HLSL uses `atan2` for the two-argument arctangent and `fmod` for modulo.
